You are auditing a code-review-readiness initiative against its authoritative plan, Plan V4 (read it in full: /home/polaris/wt/phase1/PLAN_V4_REFERENCE.md).
The ONE inviolable rule of the plan: no change may move the pipeline RACE score or faithfulness behavior; every change must be provably byte-identical/score-safe.
Two git worktrees hold the committed work:
- Phase 0 work: /home/polaris/wt/phase0 (branch chore/review-readiness-phase0, GitHub PR #1381)
- Phase 1 work: /home/polaris/wt/phase1 (branch chore/review-readiness-phase1, GitHub PR #1382)
Be EVIDENCE-BASED: run git log/ls/grep/cat, read tests and docs, and cite concrete SHAs/paths/counts. Do NOT assume something is done because a doc claims it — verify the artifact exists and matches the plan's acceptance wording. Mark PARTIAL when the artifact exists but does not fully meet the plan's stated acceptance. Report ONLY your assigned area.

YOUR AREA: Scheduled deliverables S1-S6. Start by: cd /home/polaris/wt/phase1 && git log --oneline -25 and ls the tree.
The six reviewer-axis deliverables: S1 config governance (classify+own 923 keys, precedence); S2 public-compat inventory + supported-Python/install/state-migration/rollback doc; S3 test quality/flakiness policy; S4 security/privacy review (threat model: API auth, crawler SSRF, prompt-injection, PII, log redaction, checkpoint retention); S5 operational readiness (timeouts, retries, rate/cost limits, monitoring, runbooks, recovery); S6 reproducible build (two builds hash-match, hash-pinned deps, locked env, SBOM). For each: DONE/PARTIAL/MISSING with evidence.

Return every plan item in your area with a status and concrete evidence.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Deep Cove Research — Code Review Readiness Plan (v4)
2	
3	**Goal:** make the code clean and professional enough to pass an independent Telus code review — **without ever changing the pipeline's RACE score or its faithfulness behavior.**
4	
5	**Date:** 2026-07-18 · **Status:** for owner approval (v4 — folds in three rounds of independent codex review + the exhaustive name-read worklist)
6	
7	**Version history:**
8	- v1 — first draft (research+audit workflow).
9	- v2 — codex round 1 (baseline first; "byte-identical" needs a real test; severity recalibrated; reorder).
10	- v3 — codex round 2 (baseline *manifest* + comparison protocol; config precedence/case/timing/type tests + govern-and-own keys before migrating; graph removal gated on compatibility matrix + usage evidence + repeated replays; missing axes → scheduled gated deliverables; renames gated on public-compatibility check).
11	- **v4 — codex round 3 + the name-read results:** (a) baseline manifest is **secret-safe** (digests, never raw secret values); (b) the **governing RACE/faithfulness measurement is never quarantined** — it is characterized statistically or execution stops; (c) **real owners/roles assigned**, no `TBD`; and (d) Phase 2 now carries the **concrete 210-name rename worklist** produced by reading every line of all 842 files.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## Roles (concrete — no `TBD`)
16	
17	- **Approver / risk-acceptance:** the **project owner** (you). Signs off each phase; accepts residual risk; no phase starts without approval.
18	- **Executor:** the **engineering agent (Opus), under owner supervision.** Does the work, runs the acceptance harness, produces evidence.
19	- **Independent reviewer:** **codex (GPT-5.6)** — adversarial checks at each gate.
20	
21	Every workstream below names an **Owner (Approver) + Executor**.
22	
23	---
24	
25	## The one rule (never break this)
26	
27	The pipeline's **RACE score** and its **faithfulness behavior** must stay **exactly the same** through every change. No change may move a threshold, a model name, a temperature, or a verdict. If we cannot *prove* a change is safe, we do not ship it.
28	
29	---
30	
31	## The good news: the code is better than it feels
32	
33	- **96%** of functions type-annotated · **98.5%** module-docstring coverage · **1,082 tests** exist · **secrets clean** (no keys in git) · STORM/FS-Researcher are **clean-room rewrites from the papers, cited — not copied** (a naming choice, not a legal problem).
34	
35	→ The gap is **discipline a reviewer can see, plus a few unknowns we must turn into knowns.**
36	
37	---
38	
39	## The real problems (honest severity)
40	
41	1. **HIGH — Tests not enforced.** 1,082 tests never run in CI; no lint/type-check/`pyproject.toml`; 28 CI actions on moving tags.
42	2. **HIGH — Settings scattered.** 1,707 `os.getenv` calls, ~923 keys, 24 documented; model names/numbers hardcoded in 9+ files.
43	3. **HIGH — One real architecture mess:** the live 3-way `graph.py`/`graph_v2`/`graph_v3` fork chosen by env var — the single most dangerous change.
44	4. **MEDIUM — Unprofessional naming (tidiness):** confirmed by reading every line — **210 validated renames** (see Phase 2), family leaders: `honest` ×37, version-markers ×44, `junk` ×29, `beat-both` ×12, `lethal` ×7, internal codenames ×14, cryptic abbreviations ×55.
45	5. **MEDIUM — A checkpoint built but never turned on** → every restart re-runs 30–40 min generation.
46	6. **NARROW — Docs gaps only in contract/API files** (~530 docstrings).
47	
48	**Plus six unknowns a reviewer will probe** → the Scheduled Deliverables table.
49	
50	---
51	
52	## The plan — step by step
53	
54	Referee for "did the pipeline change?": the acceptance harness (`acceptance_outline_agent.py`), governed by the Phase-0 baseline manifest + comparison protocol. The graph fork additionally needs repeated per-selector replays.
55	
56	### Phase 0 — Build a real baseline, then only the truly-safe tidy-ups
57	**Owner (Approver): project owner · Executor: engineering (Opus).**
58	
59	**0A. The reproducible baseline.**
60	
61	*0A-1 — Record a SECRET-SAFE baseline manifest* (`baseline/manifest.json`): git commit SHA; `requirements.lock` hash; Python version + OS; the provider/model routing config; seeds; exact commands; input fixtures.
62	- **Env values:** record every env-var **name** and its **non-secret** value verbatim. For any **secret** (API keys, tokens, credentials): record **only a SHA-256 digest + a "present/absent" flag — never the raw value.** The manifest carries **no plaintext secret**, is written to a git-ignored location with documented access, and is safe to share with a reviewer.
63	
64	*0A-2 — Run in an ISOLATED sandbox* (running the pipeline is **not** read-only — it writes files and calls providers): dedicated scratch output dir; GitHub backup daemon **paused**; no writes to shared state; provider calls recorded or run via the existing offline/deterministic acceptance path. Record every output artifact **and its SHA-256 hash**.
65	
66	*0A-3 — Establish non-flakiness — but NEVER quarantine the governing measurement.* Run the test suite **and** the acceptance harness **N≥3 times**; record pass/fail, timing, variance.
67	- **Only NON-governing unit tests may be quarantined** if flaky (into a tracked list).
68	- **The governing RACE score and faithfulness verdicts are NEVER quarantined.** They are **characterized statistically** — mean and spread over N repeats, with a declared equivalence band. **If they cannot be characterized within a stable band, that is a BLOCKING condition** (stop and investigate the source of variance) — not a quarantine. We do not proceed on an un-pinned oracle.
69	
70	*0A-4 — Comparison protocol* (declared before any change): "same" = the governing RACE score within the declared equivalence band over N repeats **and** identical faithfulness verdict sets on fixed inputs **and** artifact-hash match for artifacts meant to be deterministic. Document what is a **regression** vs noise.
71	
72	*0A-5 — Characterize all 3 graph selectors* on fixed inputs as **replay fixtures** (the "before" for each of v1/v2/v3).
73	
74	**0B. Changes that touch NO importable runtime code** (ship immediately): SHA-pin all 28 CI actions; fix third-party attribution; add `pyproject.toml` + CI in **report-only mode**; tidy README/architecture.
75	
76	**Deferred out of Phase 0** (only *looked* zero-risk): deleting "dead" files and changing secret types — wait for the baseline **and** the Phase-2 public-compatibility clearance.
77	
78	---
79	
80	### Phase 1 — Central settings (governance first, then full-behavior migration)
81	**Owner (Approver): project owner · Executor: engineering (Opus).**
82	
83	**1A. Govern + own the keys BEFORE moving any.** Classify **all ~923 keys** (including non-`PG_*`) as supported/internal/secret/deprecated/experimental, each with an **owner** and documented **precedence**. Gate: **100% classified + owned** before a single key migrates.
84	
85	**1B. Full-behavior characterization tests.** For every key, lock today's behavior across `{unset, empty, valid, malformed}` × `{default / .env / process-env / CLI precedence}` × `{key-case}` × `{read timing: lazy vs import-snapshot}` × `{runtime type: str / coerced / SecretStr wrapper}`. These are the real "byte-identical" proof.
86	
87	**1C. Migrate one module at a time.** Each default = today's exact value; each env name preserved; `SecretStr` for keys (call-sites updated to unwrap). Acceptance harness after each module; any deviation from the 0A baseline → stop. Never change a value in the same commit that moves it. Highest-risk: `state.py` (114 reads) and the model resolver.
88	
89	---
90	
91	### Phase 2 — Public-compatibility gate, THEN the 210-name cleanup + checkpoint
92	**Owner (Approver): project owner · Executor: engineering (Opus).**
93	
94	**2A. Public-compatibility inventory — runs BEFORE any rename/delete.** Inventory dynamic imports (`importlib`, `__import__`, `getattr` on modules, entry-points/plugins), string-based references, saved-state references, and external consumers. A name is cleared for rename/delete **only** after this shows nothing depends on it. "Zero static importers" is **not** sufficient.
95	
96	**2B. Execute the validated rename worklist** (`NAME_RENAME_WORKLIST_validated.tsv` — 210 renames, each with a risk class, produced by reading every line of all 842 files and QA'd by codex). **Execution order by risk:**
97	1. **105 `SAFE` symbol renames** (pure code symbols) — one per commit, acceptance harness before/after.
98	2. **12 `TEXT-ONLY`** (docstring/log-tag jargon — `BEAT-BOTH`, `MoST`, `GEMINI-ARCH`) — safe text edits.
99	3. **45 `FILE-RENAME`** — only after 2A clears each; `git mv` + fix importers; includes consolidating the 11 near-duplicate `run_full_scale_v10..v30` scripts into **one parameterized launcher**.
100	4. **32 `NEEDS-ALIAS`** — names that are control-surface / persisted strings (`PG_LETHAL_SEED_K`, `PG_JUNK_SOURCE_SCREEN`, `LIVE_HONEST`, `bond_analysis` dict keys, manifest keys): rename the **symbol** but **keep the old string working via an alias** — never a naive rename (would move the score).
101	- **16 `DOMAIN-REVIEW`** rows are surfaced to the owner for a keep/rename call.
102	- **135 names were validated as KEEP** (standard vocab / real version taxonomy / cited method names) — documented so we can tell Telus "checked, correct."
103	- **The `graph_v2/v3` version markers are NOT in this phase** — codex confirmed they are the real routed fork → **Phase 3**.
104	
105	**2C. Turn on the generation checkpoint.** Save **pre-check data only** (drafts, outline), never a verdict; **gate the write behind the flag** so normal runs are byte-identical; reload path re-verifies from scratch.
106	
107	---
108	
109	### Phase 3 — Docs, flakiness-gated required CI, then the graph fork (LAST)
110	**Owner (Approver): project owner · Executor: engineering (Opus).**
111	
112	**3A. Docs** — fill ~530 docstrings (contract/API first) + ADRs. Pure text.
113	
114	**3B. Flakiness policy BEFORE CI becomes required** — run the suite N times over several days; fix/quarantine flaky *non-governing* tests; define the non-flakiness bar. CI flips report-only → **required only after the suite is provably stable**, not after one green run. Add dependency hash-pinning + license/secret scanning here.
115	
116	**3C. The graph fork — fully gated.** Nothing in `graph*.py` deleted until ALL pass: (1) per-selector compatibility matrix on the 0A-5 fixtures; (2) real usage inventory (grep prod/CI/scripts/docs for `PG_GRAPH_VERSION` + `v2`/`v3` use); (3) saved-state migration fixtures for `ResearchStateV2`; (4) repeated equivalent replays per selector; (5) explicit rollback + deprecation window. Its own reviewed change.
117	
118	---
119	
120	## Scheduled deliverables — the six reviewer axes (owned, gated)
121	
122	| # | Deliverable | Acceptance | Gates (blocks) | Owner (Approver) / Executor |
123	|---|---|---|---|---|
124	| S1 | Config governance (classify+own 923 keys) | 100% classified + owned + precedence | Phase 1 migration | owner / Opus |
125	| S2 | Public-compatibility inventory + supported-Python/install/state-migration/rollback doc | inventory complete; nothing depends on renamed names | Phase 2 rename/delete | owner / Opus |
126	| S3 | Test quality / flakiness policy | suite stable across N runs; flaky (non-governing) quarantined; bar defined | Phase 3 required-CI | owner / Opus |
127	| S4 | Security / privacy review | threat-model doc (API auth, crawler SSRF, prompt-injection, PII, log redaction, checkpoint retention); no unresolved critical findings | before external exposure | owner / Opus |
128	| S5 | Operational readiness | checklist: timeouts, retries, rate/cost limits, monitoring, runbooks, recovery | before external exposure | owner / Opus |
129	| S6 | Reproducible build | two independent builds hash-match; hash-pinned deps + locked env + documented build + SBOM | before "reproducible" is claimed to Telus | owner / Opus |
130	
131	---
132	
133	## The safety rules (every change)
134	
135	1. **Baseline first** — no runtime change before the 0A secret-safe manifest + comparison protocol + N-run baseline exist, with the governing measurement statistically characterized (or execution blocked).
136	2. **Config = full-behavior proof** (value, precedence, case, coercion, malformed, timing, runtime type). Keys classified + owned before migration.
137	3. **No behavior change** in the same commit as a rename or a settings move.
138	4. **Renames/deletes gated on the public-compatibility inventory (S2)** — rename symbols/filenames only; env-var / enum / persisted string literals get an **alias**, never a naive rename.
139	5. **Checkpoint:** pre-check data only, never a verdict; write gated off by default.
140	6. **Acceptance harness before/after** every code-touching change; the graph fork additionally requires the full 3C gate.
141	7. **CI report-only → required only after the flakiness bar (S3)** — never gate cold.
142	8. **Secret-safe evidence** — no plaintext secret in any manifest/artifact; digests + access controls only.
143	9. **Do not rename real domain terms** (SmartArt, deep_crawl, OpenAlex, PubMed, Semantic Scholar) or the 135 codex-validated KEEP names. Keep paper citations.
144	
145	---
146	
147	## What to show Telus first
148	
149	1. The `pyproject.toml` + CI PR next to the **96% typing** number — quality now enforced.
150	2. The strong fundamentals, honestly (98.5% module docs, real README, 1,082 tests, pinned deps, clean secrets).
151	3. The clean-room proof for STORM/FS-Researcher.
152	4. The name-cleanup evidence (every line read; 210 scoped renames + 135 validated-correct) + this plan + the baseline manifest — proof the rest is scoped, owned, gated, and ring-fenced from the score.
153	
154	---
155	
156	## Where we start
157	
158	**Phase 0-A: the secret-safe reproducible baseline** (manifest → isolated N-run → statistically-characterized oracle → comparison protocol → 3-selector fixtures). It cannot change the pipeline, and every later "we did not move the score" claim depends on it.
159	
160	**One-line summary for the boss:** *"The pipeline is sound and the fundamentals are real; what's missing is the enforcement, the tidiness, and a few proofs an outsider needs. We establish a secret-safe evidence baseline first, then pay down each item — including 210 already-mapped renames — behind owned, gated guarantees that the score and faithfulness never move."*
161

1	# Configuration governance (Phase 1A)
2	
3	The pipeline's configuration surface, inventoried from **every** `os.getenv` / `os.environ` read site
4	in the code (not just `.env`), classified, and with the migration approach. This is the "govern before
5	migrate" gate: nothing migrates into the typed settings layer until it appears here.
6	
7	## The surface (measured)
8	- **1,644** distinct config keys read across `src/` + `scripts/` (2,592 read-sites).
9	- **1,044 runtime keys** (`src/polaris_graph`) — the governance + migration target:
10	
11	| Category | Count | Meaning | Migration priority |
12	|---|---:|---|---|
13	| model-selection | 35 | model slug / provider / LLM role choice | **P1 — the single source of truth** |
14	| secret | 97 | `*_KEY` / `*_TOKEN` / credentials | P1 — `SecretStr`, never plaintext |
15	| internal-flag | 71 | `_ENABLED`/`_MODE`/`_GATE` booleans | P2 |
16	| tuning-threshold | 372 | `MAX_`/`_CAP`/`_TIMEOUT`/`THRESHOLD`/`_K` numerics | P2 |
17	| external/system | 35 | non-`PG_` standard env (HOME, PATH, provider base urls) | keep as-is / thin wrapper |
18	| internal-other | 434 | other `PG_*` control flags | P3 (long tail) |
19	
20	- **600** keys are `scripts/`-only (dev tooling) — out of the runtime migration scope.
21	- **~1,198** keys are **code-default-only** (a literal default in the `os.getenv` call, not set in `.env`)
22	  → the migration MUST preserve each literal default exactly.
23	
24	Machine-readable inventory: `baseline/config_keys_classified.json` (git-ignored; per key: read-sites,
25	files, in-env, category, secret flag).
26	
27	## The hard rule (byte-identical by construction)
28	Every migrated key keeps its **exact `PG_*` name** and its **exact current default literal**. A
29	characterization test (Phase 1B) locks today's resolution behaviour — value, precedence, case,
30	coercion, malformed input, read-timing, runtime type — before any module migrates. Never change a
31	value in the same commit that moves it.
32	
33	## Approach (given the 1,044-key scale)
34	A one-shot rewrite of 1,044 hardcoded reads is neither safe nor reviewable. The plan is:
35	1. **Establish the pattern + the model source of truth first** — build `src/polaris_graph/settings.py`
36	   (pydantic-settings, `env_prefix="PG_"`, `SecretStr` for secrets) and migrate the **35
37	   model-selection keys** so "one file controls every model," which was the headline requirement.
38	2. **Migrate module-by-module** thereafter (start `abstractive_writer.py`), each behind a
39	   characterization test + the acceptance harness, as a tracked, gated backlog.
40	3. **A CI grep** bans *new* `os.getenv(` outside `settings.py`, so the surface stops growing while the
41	   backlog drains.
42	
43	Ownership: until per-key owners are assigned, the **project owner** is accountable for the model +
44	secret keys (P1); the executor (engineering) drains P2/P3 behind tests. No key migrates without a
45	green characterization test.
46	
47	## Progress (2026-07-19)
48	- **Done — foundation:** `settings.py` (`ModelSettings` for the 12 model keys + `resolve()` for the
49	  871-key single-default registry `config_defaults.py`), locked by characterization tests
50	  (`test_settings_models.py`, `test_config_registry.py` — resolve==os.getenv over all 871 keys).
51	- **Done — bulk call-site migration:** **832 `os.getenv("PG_X", lit)` sites across 130 modules**
52	  migrated to `resolve("PG_X")`, byte-identical, via an **AST codemod** (edits only real
53	  `os.getenv` Call nodes; strings/comments/docstrings structurally untouched; whole-file guards for
54	  any local `os`/`resolve` rebind). Verified: all 130 modules import, collection stays at the 11
55	  pre-existing baseline errors, heaviest modules' test suites behave identically to HEAD. Codex-gated
56	  (3 adversarial rounds → APPROVE).
57	- **Remaining tail (tracked, not blocking):**
58	  - **114 secret-shaped sites** → a separate `SecretStr` pass (behavior-changing; needs its own design).
59	  - **46 computed/multiline-default keys** → need typed per-domain accessors (can't be a static
60	    registry string).
61	  - **20 conflicting-default keys** → see [`config_conflicts.md`](config_conflicts.md); each needs a
62	    product decision on the single correct default before it can be centralized. One
63	    (`PG_FAITHFULNESS_NLI_THRESHOLD`, 0.65 vs 0.75) touches the frozen-faithfulness invariant.
64	  - **33 non-PG external-convention vars** (`OPENAI_*`, `HF_*`, …) — left as-is (not our namespace).
65

1	# Config finding: env vars with CONFLICTING default fallbacks
2	
3	**Status:** discovered during Phase 1C config migration (2026-07-19). Not yet resolved — each needs a product decision on the single correct default.
4	
5	These PG_ environment variables are read via `os.getenv(KEY, default)` at multiple call sites, but with **different** hardcoded fallback defaults. When the env var is unset, behavior therefore depends on *which code path* reads it first — a latent inconsistency. They were deliberately EXCLUDED from the central `resolve()` registry (which holds one default per key) because there is no single byte-identical value to adopt.
6	
7	Each must be resolved by choosing the intended default, setting it in `.env` / the registry, and collapsing all call sites to it.
8	
9	| Key | Conflicting defaults seen | Note |
10	|-----|---------------------------|------|
11	| `PG_AGENTIC_MAX_ROUNDS` | '12', '2' |  |
12	| `PG_ANALYSIS_BATCH_TIMEOUT` | '180', '240.0' |  |

17	| `PG_FETCH_CONCURRENCY` | '10', '5' |  |
18	| `PG_FETCH_TIMEOUT` | '20', '30' |  |
19	| `PG_GENERATOR_MODEL` | '', <none> |  |
20	| `PG_MAX_CITATION_FREQUENCY` | '5', '8' |  |

22	| `PG_MAX_TOTAL_ACADEMIC` | '100', '500' |  |
23	| `PG_MAX_WORDS_PER_SECTION` | '1500', '2000' |  |
24	| `PG_MIN_EVIDENCE_PER_SECTION` | '3', '5', '8' | Three distinct defaults (3/5/8) — evidence floor varies by path. |
25	| `PG_MIN_EVIDENCE_UTILIZATION` | '0.30', '0.40' | Utilization gate 0.30 vs 0.40. |
26	| `PG_MIN_QUOTE_WORDS` | '15', '5' |  |
27	| `PG_MIN_TOTAL_WORDS` | '0', '10000' | 0 vs 10000 — one path effectively disables the floor. |

"""Central, typed configuration — the single source of truth (Phase 1).

This module replaces scattered ``os.getenv`` reads with one typed settings object. Phase 1 migrates
it in slices; this first slice is **model selection** (which model each role uses), because
"one file controls every model" was the headline requirement.

Byte-identical rule: every field's default is the EXACT current literal, and every field maps to its
existing ``PG_*`` env var (via ``env_prefix``), so behaviour is unchanged. The characterization test
``tests/test_settings_models.py`` proves each field resolves identically to the current
``os.getenv(KEY, default)`` — both unset (default) and set (override).

Nothing imports this yet; call sites migrate module-by-module behind that test (see
``docs/review_readiness/config_governance.md``). The remaining model keys with multi-line/computed
defaults (``PG_CONTRADICTION_MODEL``, ``PG_ENTAILMENT_MODEL``, ``PG_RERANKER_MODEL``,
``PG_CONTENT_RELEVANCE_RERANKER_MODEL``) are added when their owning module migrates.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from pydantic import Field
from pydantic_settings import BaseSettings, SettingsConfigDict


class ModelSettings(BaseSettings):
    """Model-selection config.

    Each field reads its EXACT ``PG_*`` env var **case-sensitively** (via an explicit
    ``validation_alias``), matching ``os.getenv("PG_...")`` byte-for-byte — an ``env_prefix`` +
    case-insensitive setup would (wrongly) also match differently-cased names.
    """

    model_config = SettingsConfigDict(case_sensitive=True, extra="ignore")

    judge_model: str = Field("qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b", validation_alias="PG_JUDGE_MODEL")
    sentinel_model: str = Field("minimax/minimax-m2", validation_alias="PG_SENTINEL_MODEL")
    sweep_deepener_model: str = Field("deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro", validation_alias="PG_SWEEP_DEEPENER_MODEL")
    nli_model: str = Field("flan-t5-large", validation_alias="PG_NLI_MODEL")

    embedder_model: str = Field("", validation_alias="PG_EMBEDDER_MODEL")
    planning_gate_model: str = Field("", validation_alias="PG_PLANNING_GATE_MODEL")

=== supported_scope.md ===
# POLARIS — Supported Scope

**Version:** v1.0
**Last updated:** 2026-04-30

This document defines what research questions POLARIS will and will not accept in v1.0, and the criteria the production code uses to route each query. **All claims here are backed by code references; if the code doesn't enforce something, this document does not claim it does.**

---

## Public template surface (3 clinical variants in v1.0)

POLARIS v1.0 ships **3 curated templates** in the public `TEMPLATE_CATALOG` (`src/polaris_graph/audit_ir/template_catalog.py:520-523`):

| Template ID | Domain | Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| `v30_clinical` | Clinical (general) | Drug trial efficacy + safety, comparative therapeutics, regulatory submissions |
| `v30_clinical_oncology` | Clinical oncology | Oncology drug efficacy + safety, biomarker outcomes |
| `v30_clinical_cardio` | Clinical cardiovascular | Cardiovascular outcomes, MACE endpoints |

The 5 YAML scope templates in `config/scope_templates/` (`clinical`, `due_diligence`, `policy`, `tech`, `custom`) are **scaffolding for future expansion**. Only the 3 clinical variants above are wired through the production routing path in v1.0. `due_diligence`, `policy`, `tech`, `custom` are not exposed in `TEMPLATE_CATALOG`.

---

## In-scope research questions

The 3 v1.0 templates accept questions in their respective scopes:

### `v30_clinical` (general clinical)

Accepts:
- Drug trial efficacy comparisons (e.g., "tirzepatide vs semaglutide HbA1c outcomes in T2DM")
- Comparative therapeutics across drug classes
- Regulatory submission summaries (FDA, EMA, Health Canada, NICE, PMDA, TGA, MHRA)
- Adverse event surveillance (FAERS, EudraVigilance)
- Mechanism-of-action questions when grounded in clinical trial evidence

### `v30_clinical_oncology`

Accepts:
- Oncology drug efficacy + safety in specific tumor types

5	## Collection
6	- **16,501 tests collected** (corrects the audit's "1,082" — that was a file/subset count).
7	- **23 modules FAIL to collect.**
8	
9	### Root cause of collection failures
10	`src/polaris_graph/audit_ir/registry.py:132` runs `_RUNS = _build_runs()` **at import time**, which
11	validates that historical run-output directories exist and raises `RegistryError` when they don't
12	(e.g. `outputs/full_scale_v30_phase2_run14/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm`, absent in a clean
13	checkout). Any module importing `registry`/`live_server` then fails to import.
14	→ **Import-time filesystem validation that raises = non-portability anti-pattern.** Fix: make the
15	registry lazy (build on first use, not at import) and fail soft. (Work item, Phase 0/1.)
16	Plus 1 playwright "browser not installed" error (test-infra: needs `playwright install`).
17	
18	## Execution — clean subset (`tests/unit`, `-m "not slow and not api and not live"`, continue-on-collection-errors)
19	| Run | passed | failed | errors | wall |
20	|-----|--------|--------|--------|------|
21	| 1   | 1343   | 104    | 103    | 226.8s |
22	| 2   | 1343   | 104    | 103    | 226.1s |
23	
24	- **DETERMINISTIC** — identical across both runs → **not flaky**; failures are real, reproducible debt.
25	- Pass rate on this subset ≈ **86.6%** (1343 / 1550).
26	- `errors` (103) are mostly environment/import (playwright browser, registry chain) — setup-fixable.
27	- `failures` (104) are assertion mismatches — real test/code drift, needs triage.
28	
29	## Honest implication for Telus
30	The suite is **NOT green out-of-the-box**. Presenting "16,501 tests" without saying they don't all
31	pass would fail the first `pytest`. The fix path is clear (deterministic, not flaky): (1) make
32	`registry` import lazy, (2) `playwright install` in CI, (3) triage the 104 assertion failures.
33	This is why the plan runs the baseline before claiming anything.

items.evidence: Phase 0 added pyproject.toml, report-only CI, and requirements.lock (uv pip compile, version-pinned) — commits 0835b1a, and DRB-II lock provenance docs (6c3ea00, d8cbc0a). However: no SBOM anywhere (find for *sbom*/*.spdx*/*cyclonedx* returns nothing); requirements.lock has 0 '--hash=' lines (version-pinned, NOT hash-pinned); no CI or script performs a two-build hash-match / reproducibility check (grep of .github/ finds no reproducible/diffoscope/sbom job).
items.gap: Acceptance requires all four: two independent builds hash-match + hash-pinned deps + locked env + SBOM. Only a version-pinned lockfile exists (partial 'locked env'); the hash-pinned deps, the two-build hash-match proof, and the SBOM are all absent. 'Reproducible' cannot be claimed to Telus.

input.area_summary: Of the six scheduled reviewer-axis deliverables (S1-S6), only S1 has substantive committed work and even it is PARTIAL; S3 has a foundational baseline diagnosis (PARTIAL); S2, S4, S5, and S6 are entirely MISSING from the review-readiness initiative. Evidence base: the complete file list touched by the 26 review-readiness commits (git range 5c78cc0..HEAD on branch chore/review-readiness-phase1) contains only docs/review_readiness/{baseline_findings, baseline_test_state, config_conflicts, config_governance, gpu_fix_and_search_resolution, search_diagnosis/*} plus config code/tests — no S2/S4/S5/S6 artifact. Docs that superficially match (supported_scope.md, m26_threat_model.md, runbook.md, run_observability_monitoring.md) were all last modified 2026-07-07 by the unrelated I-deepfix-001 workstream (commit 2b42bc5) and predate the 2026-07-18 review-readiness work; none were created or updated for these deliverables. S1's classification artifact (baseline/config_keys_classified.json, 1044 keys) has zero per-key owner or precedence fields, so its 'owned + precedence' acceptance legs are unmet despite strong byte-identical migration work. The initiative is heavily concentrated on Phase 0 baseline + Phase 1 config migration; the scheduled deliverables table is almost untouched. Note the s2s3/S0-S5 commits in the log belong to a separate 'Research Planning Gate' initiative and are not these deliverables.

content.content: Structured output provided successfully
